aliases: Beef Rice Bowl with Kimchi Combo

english: Beef Rice Bowl with Kimchi Combo

food_subgroup: Rice bowl set meal with fermented vegetable side and soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for kimchi gyudon combo. FOODON_00002809 (beef rice bowl food product) is the closest parent covering the gyudon base component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers gyudon (beef bowl) as the primary component. No specific code exists for the kimchi gyudon combo set meal with egg and miso soup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for gyudon or kimchi gyudon combo set meal.

serving.description: 1 combo set (gyudon bowl + kimchi + half-boiled egg + miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard combo serving consists of approximately 550 g total, comprising a gyudon rice bowl with simmered beef and kimchi (approximately 400 g), one half-boiled egg (approximately 50 g), and a bowl of miso soup (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) with kimchi — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for beef bowl components, kimchi, soft-boiled egg, and miso soup
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for kimchi gyudon combo as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted-average composition of: (1) gyudon base (simmered beef and onion over steamed white rice) using USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked short-grain white rice (FDC ID 168878), cooked beef chuck (FDC ID 174032), and soy-mirin sauce; (2) kimchi using USDA SR Legacy fermented cabbage kimchi (FDC ID 169999); (3) half-boiled egg using USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424); (4) miso soup using USDA SR Legacy miso (FDC ID 172442) diluted to standard soup concentration. Per-100g values reflect the blended composite at the stated 550 g serving size, normalised to 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (steamed, Japanese short-grain) as primary GI anchor; composite GI estimated by weighted carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kimchi gyudon combo. Composite GI estimated using the weighted-carbohydrate method. Steamed Japanese short-grain white rice GI = 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021) contributes approximately 80% of total carbohydrate. Kimchi contributes negligible carbohydrate. Egg and miso soup contribute minimal carbohydrate. Composite GI estimated at 72 (high), consistent with white-rice-dominant Japanese rice bowl dishes. Glycemic load per 550 g serving = (72 × 107.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = approximately 77.2, rounded to 77.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, miso soup, and fermented kimchi
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 310 mg per 100 g, equating to roughly 1705 mg sodium per 550 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium soy sauce, omitting miso soup, or reducing kimchi portion can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index and high glycemic load driven by steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish because the steamed white rice base carries a high GI of approximately 72 and the full combo delivers an estimated glycemic load of 77 per serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice, reducing portion size, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and egg combined with high sodium from soy sauce, miso, and kimchi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish presents a moderate concern due to its saturated fat content from thinly sliced beef and egg (approximately 1.5 g saturated fat per 100 g) and its high sodium load; choosing a smaller beef portion, requesting low-sodium condiments, and limiting miso soup frequency can reduce cardiovascular risk while retaining the nutritional benefits of protein and fermented vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Gyudon Combo is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl set meal served at fast-casual Japanese restaurants such as Sukiya in Singapore. The dish centres on gyudon — a classic Japanese comfort food of thinly sliced beef and onion simmered in a sweet-savoury dashi broth seasoned with soy sauce, mirin, and sugar — served over a bowl of steamed short-grain white rice. The combo version adds a generous portion of kimchi (spicy Korean fermented napa cabbage), a half-boiled egg with a soft, custard-like yolk, and a bowl of miso soup, creating a satisfying and nutritionally varied set meal. The flavour profile combines the umami richness of the simmered beef with the tangy, spicy heat of kimchi and the mild, savoury depth of miso soup. Texturally, the dish contrasts the tender beef and silky egg yolk against the crunchy, acidic kimchi and the soft, sticky rice. Nutritionally, the combo provides a meaningful source of protein (approximately 43 g per serving) and iron from beef, probiotics and vitamin C from kimchi, and choline from the egg. However, the dish is high in sodium (approximately 1700 mg per serving) due to soy sauce, miso, and kimchi, and carries a high glycemic load driven by the white rice base, making it a food that individuals with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation or with dietary modifications. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore outlets due to the use of pork-derived miso and non-halal beef sourcing. It is dairy-free and contains gluten from soy sauce and miso.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kimchi gyudon combo?
What is the sodium content of gyudon?
What is in a gyudon combo set?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for kimchi gyudon combo as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for the individual components (gyudon base, kimchi, half-boiled egg, miso soup), weighted by estimated component mass fractions within the 550 g serving. GI is estimated using the weighted-carbohydrate method anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database value for steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components; no single approved-source entry for kimchi gyudon combo exists. Sentinel value -1 is not used in this record; all micronutrient fields have been estimated from component data. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Sukiya gyudon combo portion weights (gyudon bowl ~400 g, half-boiled egg ~50 g, miso soup ~100 g); actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Halal status is marked false because Sukiya Singapore outlets are not halal-certified; miso paste may contain alcohol or pork-derived ingredients. 4. GI of 72 is estimated using the weighted-carbohydrate method with steamed Japanese short-grain white rice (GI = 72, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary anchor; kimchi, egg, and miso soup contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. 5. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 107.25) / 100 = 77.2, rounded to 77.0; available carbohydrate per serving = 19.5 g/100 g × 550 g / 100 = 107.25 g. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are used as closest-parent anchors; no exact ontology code exists for this composite dish. 7. Cuisine tags from the input record (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Fried Chicken) appear to be restaurant-level tags unrelated to this specific dish and have not been carried forward. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins and minerals are estimated from USDA component data and are not confirmed zero; no -1 sentinel values are used in this record.
